sanskrit_devanagari:
इन्द्रियाणि मनो बुद्धिरस्याधिष्ठानमुच्यते ।
एतैर्विमोहयत्येष ज्ञानमावृत्य देहिनम् ॥ ४० ॥
sanskrit_iast:
indriyāṇi mano buddhir
asyādhiṣṭhānam ucyate |
etair vimohayaty eṣa
jñānam āvṛtya dehinam || 40 ||

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_03.040 = MBh_06,025.040). Body text identical; word-segmentation differs by edition.

translations.verbatim_quote: The senses, the mind and the intelligence are the sitting places of this lust. Through them lust covers the real knowledge of the living entity and bewilders him.
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The enemy has captured different strategic positions in the body of the conditioned soul, and therefore Lord Kṛṣṇa is giving hints of those places, so that one who wants to conquer the enemy may know where he can be found.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.40 Indriyani, the organs; manah, mind; and buddhih, the intellect; ucyate, are said to be; asya, its, desire’s; adhisthanam, abode. Esah, this one, desire; vimohayati, diversely deludes; dehinam, the embodied being; avrtya, by veiling; jnanam, Knowledg; etaih, with the help of t
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: indriyāṇi mano buddhiḥ — the senses, mind, and intellect — asya adhiṣṭhānam ucyate — are said to be its (kāma's) seat. etaiḥ vimohayati eṣa — through these it deludes — jñānam āvṛtya dehinam — covering knowledge in the embodied one. The verse names the three-layer architecture through which kāma operates and sets up the hierarchy of 3.42.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.40 The senses, the mind and the intellect are the instruments of desire in so far as it overpowers the self through them. By means of these, viz., the senses, the mind and the intellect, which have been reduced to the position of servants through attachment to sense objects, des
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: kāma's working location is the triple system of senses, mind, and intellect. Through these together it covers the discriminating function (jñāna) and deludes the dehī. The doctrinal claim: the obstacle has a specific layered structure, and the remedy must address each layer in turn.

positions.rendering: the senses, the mind and the intelligence are the sitting places of this lust. Through them lust covers the real knowledge of the living entity and bewilders him
the senses, mind, and intellect are said to be its dwelling places; through them, desire covers one's knowledge and deludes the embodied soul
the three — indriyāṇi, manas, buddhi — are kāma's adhiṣṭhānam (seat). Through these three layers, kāma operates. The verse establishes the layered structure that 3.42 will explicitly hierarchise
kāma's adhiṣṭhānam — its working location — comprises the senses, mind, and intellect. Through these three together it deludes the dehī. The verse prepares the explicit hierarchy of 3.42

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both commentators read the three loci as a layered architecture (sense-input, attentional layer, discriminating layer). The verse is doctrinally important because it sets up 3.42's explicit hierarchisation. Translators preserve the three loci but sometimes flatten them to a list rather than a layered structure.

literal_meaning: The senses, the mind, and the buddhi (intellect) are said to be its (kāma's) seat. Through these it deludes the embodied one (dehī), covering knowledge.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names the structural location of kāma. It sits in three places: the senses (indriyāṇi), the mind (manas), and the intellect (buddhi). Through these three layers it deludes the dehī — the embodied actor — by covering jñāna. The three-layer diagnosis is doctrinal: the obstacle is not unitary; it operates through a specific layered architecture, and the remedy must be applied at the right layer.
